Description: Employees or teams using AI tools without governance or security oversight.
Organizations deploying AI without formal governance policies or oversight mechanisms.
Industrial or enterprise AI projects slowed by unclear or evolving regulatory requirements.
AI solutions needing extensive documentation and traceability to meet industry certification requirements.
Risks related to compliance with privacy regulations when processing sensitive data with AI.
Large AI models require significant compute resources and energy
Difficulty embedding large generative models directly on devices such as robots due to hardware limitations.
Reliance on hybrid cloud architectures to compensate for limited on-device processing power.
AI systems forgetting earlier information when processing large files or datasets.
AI generating incorrect statistical conclusions when analyzing large datasets.
AI systems frequently misinterpreting user instructions or prompts.
Users needing many prompt iterations to achieve acceptable results.
Generated avatars showing unnatural facial expressions or robotic behaviors.
Objects or characters changing appearance across frames in AI-generated video.
Generated video motion sometimes defying realistic physical behavior.
Video generation tools limiting output duration requiring manual assembly of longer content.
AI failing to generate outputs faithful to the prompt description.
Iterative prompting and experimentation consuming credits and increasing costs significantly.
Generated code often containing errors that the AI struggles to correct.
Automatically generated code lacking structure or documentation leading to long-term maintenance issues.
Loss of code access or functionality if AI development platforms discontinue services.
No-code platforms sometimes lacking mechanisms to export or back up generated applications.

Problem: Lack of AI literacy in organizations
Description: Decision-makers lacking understanding of AI capabilities and limitations.
People assuming basic library knowledge is sufficient for AI expertise.
Companies unsure where to start or how to structure AI initiatives.
Organizations lacking proper internal processes to organize and manage data.
Smaller companies struggling to gain trust when offering AI solutions compared to large enterprises.
Fear among employees that AI may replace their jobs leading to reluctance in adoption.
Organizations concerned about deploying AI systems that may produce incorrect outputs.

Problem: Internal misalignment between leadership and teams
Description: Executives pushing AI adoption while operational teams resist or lack ownership.

Problem: Misalignment between AI marketing and real capability
Description: Overhyped expectations created by AI marketing narratives.
Belief that agents operate fully autonomously without supervision or guardrails.
Organizations struggling to integrate probabilistic AI systems into deterministic workflows.
AI reducing time-based consulting work and forcing shifts to value-based or subscription pricing models.
AI systems requiring significant infrastructure expertise to scale to production workloads.
Attackers manipulating prompts to force AI agents to execute unintended actions.
Malicious inputs redirecting autonomous agents to perform unintended tasks.
AI development environments enabling attackers to execute arbitrary code.

Problem: Supply chain vulnerabilities in AI tools
Description: Malicious packages or plugins compromising AI development environments.
Agents accessing and leaking sensitive data through integrations or tools.
Agents exploiting excessive permissions to access unauthorized systems or data.
Agents performing unintended or harmful actions without human oversight.
Users trusting AI outputs excessively

Problem: Lack of observability in AI systems
Description: Limited monitoring tools to track AI outputs and behaviors in production.
Difficulty identifying when AI outputs are incorrect or hallucinated.
